infobox.value: [N-VA](/wiki/New_Flemish_Alliance "New Flemish Alliance"), [Open Vld](/wiki/Open_Flemish_Liberals_and_Democrats "Open Flemish Liberals and Democrats")

(Dutch: [[liːr]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Lier.ogg "File:Nl-Lier.ogg"); [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Lierre* [[ljɛʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-WikiLucas00-lierre.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-WikiLucas00-lierre.wav")) is a [municipality](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") located in the [Belgian](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") province of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp_(province) "Antwerp (province)"). It is composed of the city of Lier proper and the village of [Koningshooikt](/wiki/Koningshooikt "Koningshooikt"). The city centre is surrounded by the river *Nete*, around which it grew. In 2018, Lier had a total population of 35,712. The total area is 49.70 km2 making a [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") (PD) of 720 per km2. Lier is known for its beers (which include [Caves](/wiki/Caves_(beer) "Caves (beer)")), its patron saint [St. Gummarus](/wiki/Gummarus "Gummarus") and *Lierse vlaaikes* cake. It is also home to the world headquarters of [Van Hool](/wiki/Van_Hool "Van Hool"), a global bus and coach manufacturer. Lier's two principal [football clubs](/wiki/Football_club "Football club") are [K. Lyra-Lierse](/wiki/K_Lyra-Lierse_Berlaar "K Lyra-Lierse Berlaar") and [K. Lierse S.K.](/wiki/K._Lierse_S.K. "K. Lierse S.K.")

**Lier** (Dutch: [[liːr]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Lier.ogg "File:Nl-Lier.ogg"); [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Lierre* [[ljɛʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-WikiLucas00-lierre.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-WikiLucas00-lierre.wav")) is a [municipality](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") located in the [Belgian](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") province of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp_(province) "Antwerp (province)"). It is composed of the city of Lier proper and the village of [Koningshooikt](/wiki/Koningshooikt "Koningshooikt"). The city centre is surrounded by the river *Nete*, around which it grew. In 2018, Lier had a total population of 35,712. The total area is 49.70 km2 making a [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") (PD) of 720 per km2. Lier is known for its beers (which include [Caves](/wiki/Caves_(beer) "Caves (beer)")), its patron saint [St. Gummarus](/wiki/Gummarus "Gummarus") and *Lierse vlaaikes* cake. It is also home to the world headquarters of [Van Hool](/wiki/Van_Hool "Van Hool"), a global bus and coach manufacturer. Lier's two principal [football clubs](/wiki/Football_club "Football club") are [K. Lyra-Lierse](/wiki/K_Lyra-Lierse_Berlaar "K Lyra-Lierse Berlaar") and [K. Lierse S.K.](/wiki/K._Lierse_S.K. "K. Lierse S.K.")

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lier,_Belgium&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The etymology of the name *Lier* is still under debate. It most likely refers to the river *Nete* and the muddy soils that surround it. The Latin name of Lier is *Lyra*, the suffix of which (-ara) is probably derived from the Germanic or Celtic reference to *river*. Alternatively, the origin might be the Germanic words *Ledo* or *Ledi*, which both refer to a location near the confluence of rivers (in this case the *Minor and Major [Nete](/wiki/Nete_(river) "Nete (river)")*). Other explanations include the old Dutch word *laar* (clearance in the woods) or the word *liere* (parapet). There is also a resemblance to the Swedish word *leira*, which means *muddy shore*, or the Icelandic word *leir*, which means *clay*. An all together different explanation is the Germanic word *hieura*, which refers to a hillock type of country.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lier,_Belgium&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Lier%2C_Belgium_%3B_Ferraris_Map.jpg/250px-Lier%2C_Belgium_%3B_Ferraris_Map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lier,_Belgium_;_Ferraris_Map.jpg)

Lier on the [Ferraris map](/wiki/Ferraris_map "Ferraris map") (around 1775)

There is scant record of Lier predating the 7th century. [Saint Gummarus](/wiki/Gummarus "Gummarus") himself was born in the 7th century and died on 11 October 714. He was canonised in 754.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In 1194 Lier was given [oppidum](/wiki/Oppidum "Oppidum") status and in 1212 granted municipal rights.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The Lier [beguinage](/wiki/Beguinage "Beguinage") was founded in 1258 and in 1998 was inscribed on the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_List "World Heritage List") as part of the [Flemish Béguinages](/wiki/Flemish_B%C3%A9guinages "Flemish Béguinages") site.[[5]](#cite_note-unesco1-5) The last surviving beguine died in 1994.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Oud_Kerkhof_nrs_1_2_en_3_in_het_Begijnhof_te_Lier.jpg/250px-Oud_Kerkhof_nrs_1_2_en_3_in_het_Begijnhof_te_Lier.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oud_Kerkhof_nrs_1_2_en_3_in_het_Begijnhof_te_Lier.jpg)

[Beguinage](/wiki/Beguinage "Beguinage"), a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site")

In the 14th century, Duke [Jan II](/wiki/John_II,_Duke_of_Brabant "John II, Duke of Brabant") wished to reward the City of Lier for joining his fight against the [City of Mechelen](/wiki/Mechelen "Mechelen"). He offered the city the choice of either hosting a university or a livestock market. The city notoriously selected the livestock market option, upon which the Duke is reported to have sighed: "Oh, those wretched *sheep heads*". Today, a herd of bronze sheep near the [Zimmer tower](/wiki/Zimmer_tower "Zimmer tower") serves as a reminder to this fateful decision. The [university](/wiki/Katholieke_Universiteit,_Leuven "Katholieke Universiteit, Leuven") was eventually seeded in the city of [Leuven](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven"), in 1425, as one of Europe's first and today most prominent universities. The nickname *Schapekoppen* (which translates to *Sheep Heads*) is still used in Flanders to refer to inhabitants of Lier.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In 1496, Lier was the scene of the marriage between [Philip the Handsome](/wiki/Philip_I_of_Castile "Philip I of Castile"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) son of [Maximilian of Austria](/wiki/Maximilian_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Maximilian I, Holy Roman Emperor"), and [Joanna of Castile](/wiki/Joanna_of_Castile "Joanna of Castile"). This marriage was pivotal to the history of Europe, for [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor"), who was born to this marriage ([Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), 1500), would go on to rule both the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") and the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire").

King [Christian II of Denmark](/wiki/Christian_II_of_Denmark "Christian II of Denmark"), accompanied by his spouse [Isabella](/wiki/Isabella_of_Austria "Isabella of Austria") (sister to Charles V and known as *Queen Elisabeth*), lived in Lier until 1523, after having been expelled from Denmark by the local nobility while waiting in vain for military support from his brother in law.[[9]](#cite_note-9) He attempted again to regain the Danish and Norwegian throne, but was taken prisoner and spent the rest of his life detained in the Danish castles of Sønderborg and Kalundborg. Isabella died in 1526 at the Castle of [Zwijnaarde](/wiki/Zwijnaarde "Zwijnaarde").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Heksensteen.jpg/250px-Heksensteen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heksensteen.jpg)

"Witches stone" on the marketplace

A conspicuous feature of the market square is a headstone (witches-stone, placed in 1974) that marks the spot where Lier's last witchcraft-related execution is traditionally believed to have taken place.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1860, a [skeleton of a mammoth](/wiki/Lier_mammoth "Lier mammoth") was unearthed at the site of the current city hall buildings.[[12]](#cite_note-12) It was the first mammoth skeleton to be discovered in western Europe.

At the start of the WWI, [King Albert](/wiki/King_Albert_I "King Albert I") and his Chiefs of Staff were temporarily headquartered in Lier, before retreating to [Temse](/wiki/Temse "Temse") as German lines advanced. Being part of the [redoubt of Antwerp](/wiki/National_Redoubt_(Belgium) "National Redoubt (Belgium)"), the city suffered heavily under German artillery fire, leaving much of its medieval structures damaged beyond repair. Most medieval-style structures that exist today (including the town hall and its belfry) are therefore replicas, built shortly after the War.

Lier is routinely referred to with the rhyme *Lierke Plezierke* (which roughly translates as "fun in Lier"). This expression originates from a booklet [Felix Timmermans](/wiki/Felix_Timmermans "Felix Timmermans") wrote in 1928, in celebration of the 50th wedding anniversary of a neighbouring couple.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The strong ties Lier had developed with the Belgian military ever since in 1888 artillery barracks were built, continued after the World Wars. From 1955 to 1997 the barracks housed the Royal School of Cadets (cadet school for the Belgian army).[[14]](#cite_note-14) A prominent alumnus of this school is Belgian astronaut [Frank De Winne](/wiki/Frank_De_Winne "Frank De Winne"). The site was vacated by the army in 1998, acquired by the city, and now houses city hall and the police department. Two artillery pieces are on display on the city hall's patio, as a reminder of the building's past.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The city of Lier is also patron city to the Belgian Army's *Second Artillery Regiment* (now termed the *Artillery Battalion*).

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lier,_Belgium&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

Some might argue that Lier is a part of the Antwerp suburban area, making it a district on itself, but generally, Lier is divided into five main districts: Leuvensepoort, Mechelpoort, Lisp, the city centre and [Koningshooikt](/wiki/Koningshooikt "Koningshooikt"). The last is a historic village that was administratively joined to the town as a [sub-municipality](/wiki/Deelgemeente "Deelgemeente") in 1977.

## Points of interest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lier,_Belgium&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Points of interest")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/David_Roberts_%281796-1864%29_-_Lierre%2C_Interior_of_Saint-Gommaire_-_P258_-_The_Wallace_Collection.jpg/250px-David_Roberts_%281796-1864%29_-_Lierre%2C_Interior_of_Saint-Gommaire_-_P258_-_The_Wallace_Collection.jpg)](/wiki/File:David_Roberts_(1796-1864)_-_Lierre,_Interior_of_Saint-Gommaire_-_P258_-_The_Wallace_Collection.jpg)

*[Interior of Saint-Gommaire, Lierre](/wiki/Interior_of_Saint-Gommaire,_Lierre "Interior of Saint-Gommaire, Lierre")* by [David Roberts](/wiki/David_Roberts_(painter) "David Roberts (painter)"), 1850

* The [Beguinage](/wiki/Beguinage "Beguinage") ([UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site")) and [St Margarita church](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sint-Margaritakerk_(Lier) "nl:Sint-Margaritakerk (Lier)"), 17th century.
* The [St. Gummarus Church](/wiki/Gummarus "Gummarus"), [Gothic architecture](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture"), 14th century; under high altar is grave of [Olav Engelbrektsson](/wiki/Olav_Engelbrektsson "Olav Engelbrektsson"), last Roman Catholic [Archbishop of Nidaros](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Nidaros "Archdiocese of Nidaros").
* The [Timmermans - Opsomerhuis](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Museum_Timmermans-Opsomerhuis "nl:Museum Timmermans-Opsomerhuis") museum.
* The [Stedelijk Museum Wuyts-Van Campen en Baron Caroly](/wiki/Stedelijk_Museum_Wuyts-Van_Campen_en_Baron_Caroly "Stedelijk Museum Wuyts-Van Campen en Baron Caroly"), a fine arts museum.
* The [Town hall](/wiki/Town_hall "Town hall"), [rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo") architecture, 18th century.
  + The adjoining 14th century [belfry](/wiki/Belfry_(architecture) "Belfry (architecture)"), listed in the UNESCO World Heritage Site [Belfries of Belgium and France](/wiki/Belfries_of_Belgium_and_France "Belfries of Belgium and France").[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* The [Zimmer Tower](/wiki/Zimmer_Tower "Zimmer Tower") houses a unique astronomical clock built in the 1930s, by Louis Zimmer.

### In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lier,_Belgium&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

In 2004, Lier was the host for the third episode of *[Fata Morgana](/wiki/Fata_Morgana_(game_show) "Fata Morgana (game show)")*, the TV show in which Belgian celebrities gave a city or town several challenges. The theme was "Lier 2020" and the inhabitants succeeded in completing all challenges.
